div.head: [Adhyؤپya 1]
div.note: MS Kathmandu KL 699 (K) is a witness only for 3.1.1 below.
MS Kathmandu NAK 1-1079 (N) is a witness only up to 3.2.35, at which point the scribe stopped writing in mid-folio.

list.item: Witness K breaks off here, resuming only in the second chapter of the Cikitsؤپsthؤپna.

div.l:
cf. 1938 ed. 3.1.3
sarvvabhإ«tؤپnؤپل¹… kؤپraل¹‡am akؤپraل¹‡aل¹ƒ sattvarajastamo lakل¹£aل¹‡am aل¹£ل¹­arإ«pam akhilasyؤپsya jagataل¸¥ saل¹ƒbhavahetur avyaktan nؤپma | tad ekam anekeل¹£ؤپل¹ƒ kل¹£etrajأ±ؤپnؤپm adhiل¹£ل¹­hؤپnaل¹ƒ samudra ivodakaukasؤپل¹ƒ bhؤپvؤپnؤپm ||

list.item: Witnesses N and H read samudra ivodakaujasؤپل¹ƒ bhؤپvؤپnؤپm, which is linguistically and semantically implausible. It might mean "like the ocean is a support for creatures that take their strength (ojas) from water." But the simile and the language remain obscure and unlikely. Philipp Maas has suggested the minimal emendation to ivodakaukasؤپل¹ƒ which is compelling semantically and palaeographically. It relies only on the confusion of the Nepalese characters ً‘گ– and ً‘گژ.

div.l:

cf. 1938 ed. 3.1.7
svaل¸¥ svaإ› caiل¹£ؤپل¹ƒ viل¹£ayo 'dhibhإ«tam | svayam adhyؤپtmam | adhidevan tu | atha buddher brahmؤپ | ahaل¹ƒkؤپrasyeإ›varaل¸¥ | manasaإ› candramؤپل¸¥ | diإ›aل¸¥ إ›rotrasya | tvaco vؤپyuل¸¥ | sإ«ryaإ› cakل¹£uل¹£aل¸¥ | rasanasyؤپpaل¸¥ | pل¹›thivؤ« ghrؤپل¹‡asya | vؤپco 'gniل¸¥ | hastayor indraل¸¥ | pؤپdayor vviل¹£ل¹‡uل¸¥ | pؤپyor mmitraل¸¥ | prajؤپpatir upasthasyeti |

list.item: We emend to the vulgate text, especially in the light of the other very rare instances of these parallels in Sanskrit literature. It is hard to make grammatical or semantic sense of the Nepalese readings svaل¹ƒ svaل¹ƒ svaiل¹£ؤپm (or إ›caiل¹£ؤپm or scaiل¹£ؤپm). Perhaps the original reading dhibhإ«tam was first mistaken to be vibhإ«tam. This is a plausible error since dhi and vi are similar in early Nepalese orthography. Subsequently, a scribe might have normalized vibhإ«tam to vibhutvam in order to make sense in the light of the following list of deities. If these conjectures are correct, these changes must have happened very early in the transmission, in an ancestor manuscript of both witnesses K and H. The word vibhutva occurs in Sؤپل¹…khyakؤپrikؤپ 42, although in a quite different context.
Parallel to the homologies described in Mahؤپbhؤپrata 14.42.27â€“40, which relate ontological triples.
The tu is awkward. Witnesses N and H have daل¹‡ل¸چas after the tu.

div.l:
cf. 1938 ed. 3.1.8

div.l:
cf. 1938 ed. 3.1.9
ata إ«rdhvam prakل¹›tipuruل¹£ayoل¸¥ sؤپdharmmyavaidharmmye vyؤپkhyؤپsyؤپmaل¸¥ || ubhؤپv apy anؤپdؤ« ubhؤپv apy anantau | ubhؤپv api nityau | ubhؤپv apy aparau ubhؤپv apy aliل¹…gau ubhau ca sarvvagatؤپv iti || ekؤپ tu prakل¹›tir acetanؤپ triguل¹‡ؤپ bؤ«jadharmmiل¹‡ؤ« prasavadharmminؤ« amadhyasthadharmmiل¹‡ؤ« ceti | bahavas tu puruل¹£ؤپإ› cetanؤپvantaل¸¥ | aguل¹‡ؤپ abؤ«jadharmmiل¹‡o 'prasavadharmmiل¹‡o madhyasthadharmmiل¹‡aإ› ceti |

list.item: It is unexpected to assert, as the Nepalese witnesses do, that Puruل¹£a and Prakل¹›ti are impermanent. Perhaps at an earlier point in the text's transmission, a scribe improperly repeated the initial alpha-privative that is used in every other qualifier in the list. This emendation is influenced by the very similar wording of Mahؤپbhؤپrata إڑؤپntiparvan 210.8ab: ubhau nityau .... (see note on testimonia). Note also that the sequence of qualifiers in the Nepalese manuscripts is different than the vulgate: anitya-apara-aliل¹…ga rather than aliل¹…ga-nitya-anapara.
Note the sandhi hiatus, -dharmiل¹‡ؤ« - amadhya-
The daل¹‡ل¸چa here breaks the sentence but disambiguates the sandhi.
Cf. Mahؤپbhؤپrata إڑؤپntiparvan 210.7cd-8ab: anؤپdyantؤپv ubhؤپv etؤپv aliل¹…gau cؤپpy ubhؤپv api // 7 ubhau nityau sإ«kل¹£matarau mahadbhyaإ› ca mahattarau /.

list.item: Witness N is faint and written close to the abraded top of the folio so that superscript vowels are uncertain. Witness H has a scribal correction of the vowel i. The reading adopted here, with a feminine sentence subject, is neither the Nepalese nor the vulgate and is almost certainly unoriginal. Without further evidence it is not possible to recover the oldest reading.
